teaching.title: The C++ Program Skeleton
teaching.analogy: A C++ program is like C's program but with upgrades installed. The foundation (#include) is still there, but now you get a more powerful output system (cout) and the whole standard library at your fingertips via 'using namespace std'.
teaching.content: C++ keeps C's structure but adds powerful features. The skeleton: #include <iostream> for input/output, optional 'using namespace std;' for convenience, int main() entry point, and cout for output instead of printf.

examples.title: Minimal C++ Program
examples.explanation: Very similar to C but with iostream and cout.

examples.title: C vs C++ Output
examples.explanation: cout with << is C++'s way — cleaner than printf!

teaching.funFact: C++ was created by Bjarne Stroustrup in 1983 as 'C with Classes'. The ++ in C++ is literally the increment operator — meaning C++ is 'one better than C'! 🚀

teaching.title: cout & endl — C++'s Output System
teaching.analogy: cout is C++'s output stream. The << operator is like a pipeline arrow — it pushes data from right to left into cout. endl flushes the stream and adds a newline. Think of it as: cout (screen) ← data ← data ← data.
teaching.content: cout (character output stream) is C++'s standard output. Use << to send values. Chain multiple << to print multiple items. endl adds a newline and flushes the buffer. '\n' is faster than endl (no flush).

examples.title: Basic cout Usage
examples.explanation: Chain << to combine multiple values.

examples.title: endl vs \n
examples.explanation: Both add a newline. endl also flushes the buffer.
examples.code:
cout << "Fast" << '\n';  // faster, no flush
cout << "Safe" << endl;  // slower, flushes buffer

teaching.funFact: cout is part of std (the standard namespace). Without 'using namespace std;', you'd write std::cout — the :: is the scope resolution operator. Many professional C++ codebases use std::cout explicitly! 🎯

teaching.title: namespace std & #include
teaching.analogy: 'using namespace std' is like moving into a new city and learning all the local shortcuts. Without it, you must say the full address (std::cout, std::endl). With it, you can just say cout and endl — the compiler knows where to find them.
teaching.content: #include <iostream> loads the input/output stream library. 'using namespace std;' brings all standard library names into scope so you can write cout instead of std::cout. Both are C++ — only #include exists in C.

examples.title: With vs Without using namespace
examples.explanation: Both approaches are valid C++.

teaching.funFact: The C++ standard library (STL) lives in the 'std' namespace. Using 'using namespace std;' in header files is considered bad practice — it pollutes the namespace for everyone who includes that file! ⚠️

mcq.q: Which header provides cout and cin in C++?
mcq.explanation: Header Intel: <iostream> is the C++ input/output stream library. It provides cout (output), cin (input), and cerr (error output). <stdio.h> is C's version — they coexist but don't mix well.

mcq.q: What does 'using namespace std;' do?
options: Allows writing cout instead of std::cout
mcq.explanation: Namespace: std is the namespace where cout, cin, endl, string etc. live. 'using namespace std;' lets you write the short names. Without it, you'd need std::cout, std::endl every time.

mcq.q: What operator sends data to cout in C++?
mcq.explanation: Stream Operator: << is the insertion operator for output streams. It pushes data into cout. >> is the extraction operator used with cin for input.

mcq.q: What does endl do when used with cout?
options: Adds a newline AND flushes the output buffer
mcq.explanation: endl: Inserts a newline character AND flushes the output buffer (forces immediate display). '\n' only adds a newline without flushing — faster but may delay display.

mcq.q: Which is the correct C++ output for multiple values?
mcq.explanation: Chaining: cout << value1 << value2 chains multiple values with the << operator. Each << pushes another piece of data into the output stream.

coding.title: The C++ Header
coding.objective: What header file provides cout and cin in C++?
coding.hint: input-output stream — no .h for modern C++ standard headers.

coding.title: Output Operator
coding.objective: What operator sends data to cout?
coding.hint: Two less-than signs — the insertion operator.

coding.title: Namespace Shortcut
coding.objective: What statement lets you write 'cout' instead of 'std::cout'?

options: Missing 'using namespace std;' — without it, write std::cout and std::endl
debugging.answer: Missing 'using namespace std;' — without it, write std::cout and std::endl
debugging.hint: Without 'using namespace std;', you must qualify cout as std::cout.

options: Hello should be in single quotes
debugging.hint: << inserts INTO cout (output). >> extracts FROM cin (input). Don't mix them up!
<stdio.h> is C's I/O library. C++'s cout lives in <iostream>.

prediction.explanation: Three values chained with <<: "C++", " ", "Rocks". They print consecutively: C++ Rocks. endl adds a newline.
The first two couts have no endl or \n, so no newline. All three print on the same line: ABC. Only the last endl moves to a new line.

teaching.title: Variables in C++ — Types & auto
teaching.analogy: C++ inherited C's strict type system but added a smart upgrade: 'auto'. With auto, C++ can figure out the type from the value you provide — like a smart assistant who infers what container you need based on what you're putting in.
teaching.content: C++ uses the same typed variable declarations as C: int, double, char, bool. But C++11 introduced 'auto' — the compiler infers the type. You still need to initialize auto variables so the compiler can determine the type.

examples.title: Standard Declarations
examples.explanation: Same as C — explicit type names.

examples.title: Using auto
examples.explanation: auto deduces the type from the assigned value.
examples.code:
auto x = 5;         // int (deduced)
auto pi = 3.14;     // double (deduced)
auto name = "Neo";  // const char* (careful!)
auto flag = true;   // bool (deduced)

teaching.funFact: C++'s 'auto' keyword predates C++11 and meant something completely different (automatic storage). It was repurposed in C++11 for type deduction — a rare case of a keyword getting a new meaning! 🔄

teaching.title: string — C++'s Proper String Type
teaching.analogy: C uses char arrays for text — like storing words by manually placing each letter in a filing cabinet. C++ gives you std::string — a smart shelf that grows, shrinks, and manages itself automatically. Same result, dramaticaly easier to use!
teaching.content: C++ has a proper string type: std::string (from <string>). Unlike C's char arrays, strings can be concatenated with +, compared with ==, and have many built-in methods. Include <string> (or iostream already brings it).

examples.title: string vs char[]
examples.explanation: C++ string is far more user-friendly than C's char[].

examples.title: String Operations
examples.explanation: string supports intuitive operators.

teaching.funFact: C++ string handles memory automatically — it grows when you add content and frees memory when it goes out of scope. C's char arrays have FIXED sizes. Buffer overflow is one of the most exploited security vulnerabilities in C! 🔐

mcq.q: What does 'auto x = 5;' declare?
options: An int variable — compiler deduces type from value 5
mcq.explanation: Type Deduction: auto tells the compiler to figure out the type from the assigned value. 5 is an integer literal, so x becomes int. auto pi = 3.14 makes pi a double.

mcq.q: Which C++ type should you use for text instead of char[]?
mcq.explanation: Modern String: Use std::string for text in C++. It's safer, resizable, supports + concatenation and == comparison. char[] is C-style and requires manual memory management.

mcq.q: What is output of: string s = "Hi"; cout << s.length();
mcq.explanation: String Method: .length() returns the number of characters in the string. "Hi" has 2 characters: H and i.

mcq.q: What does 'string a = "Code"; string b = a + "Clash";' produce?
options: Code in a, Clash in b
mcq.explanation: String Concatenation: C++ string supports + operator for joining strings. a + "Clash" creates a new string "CodeClash" stored in b.

mcq.q: C++ string comparison: does (name == "Alex") work correctly?
options: Yes — == compares string content in C++
mcq.explanation: C++ String ==: Unlike Java (where == compares references), C++ string's == operator compares the actual content. name == "Alex" returns true if the characters match.

coding.title: Auto Declaration
coding.objective: What keyword lets C++ deduce the type automatically?
coding.hint: C++11 keyword — compiler figures out the type from the value.

coding.title: C++ String Type
coding.objective: What is the C++ type for text that supports + and ==?
coding.hint: Not char[] — it's the C++ standard library string type.

coding.title: String Length
coding.objective: What method returns the number of characters in a C++ string?
coding.hint: Same as Java's String method — counts the characters.

options: auto variables MUST be initialized — the compiler needs a value to deduce the type
x is an invalid name with auto
debugging.answer: auto variables MUST be initialized — the compiler needs a value to deduce the type
debugging.hint: auto deduces type FROM the value. Without a value, there's nothing to deduce from!

options: .equals() is a Java method. In C++, use == operator: if (name == "Alex")
debugging.answer: .equals() is a Java method. In C++, use == operator: if (name == "Alex")
debugging.hint: C++ string uses == for comparison. .equals() is Java. Don't confuse the two!

prediction.explanation: a is int (5), b is double (2.5). Mixed arithmetic promotes to double: 5 + 2.5 = 7.5.

teaching.title: C++ Primitive Types
teaching.analogy: C++ inherits all of C's types but adds native bool. Think of C++'s type system as C's toolbox plus a few new tools that make life easier — especially bool (true/false) and string.
teaching.content: C++ has all C types (int, float, double, char) plus a native bool type. bool stores true or false (lowercase). C++ also has long, short, unsigned variants. Unlike C, you don't need <stdbool.h> — bool is built-in.

examples.title: C++ Core Types
examples.explanation: Includes bool natively — unlike C.
examples.code:
int score = 100;         // 4 bytes
double price = 9.99;     // 8 bytes
bool isAlive = true;     // true/false (native!)
char grade = 'A';        // 1 byte
string name = "Alex";   // dynamic size

examples.title: bool in C++
examples.explanation: C++ bool prints as 1/0 by default.

teaching.funFact: cout prints bool as 1 or 0 by default. Use 'cout << boolalpha' to make it print 'true'/'false'. 'boolalpha' is a stream manipulator from <iostream>! 🎛️

teaching.title: Type Comparison: Python vs Java vs C vs C++
teaching.analogy: Each language has its own flavor of the same basic types. Python infers everything. Java and C++ require declarations. C lacks bool and uses char arrays for strings. C++ bridges C's power with modern convenience.
teaching.content: Understanding the differences across languages reveals the design philosophy of each. C++ adds bool and string to C, making it more developer-friendly while keeping C's raw speed and control.

examples.title: Type Comparison Table
examples.explanation: Same concept, different syntax across languages.

teaching.funFact: C++ is one of the only languages where you can write both high-level code (like Python) and low-level system code (like C) in the same file. This makes it ideal for game engines and operating systems! 🎮

mcq.q: Does C++ have a native bool type?
mcq.explanation: Native Bool: Yes! C++ has a built-in bool type with true and false keywords (all lowercase). This is unlike C, which required <stdbool.h> or using int values.

mcq.q: What does 'cout << true;' print by default in C++?
mcq.explanation: Bool Output: By default, cout prints bool as 1 (true) or 0 (false). To print 'true'/'false', use the boolalpha manipulator: cout << boolalpha << true.

mcq.q: Which is the correct way to declare a string in C++?
mcq.explanation: C++ String: string (lowercase) is the C++ standard type. String (uppercase) is Java. char name[] is C-style. In C++, use string — it's safer and easier.

mcq.q: What is the size of bool in C++?
mcq.explanation: Size Intel: bool is typically 1 byte in C++, even though only 1 bit is needed. Most CPUs work with bytes as the minimum addressable unit, so 1 byte is the practical minimum.

mcq.q: C++ string vs C char[] — which is dynamically sized?
options: string is dynamic — it resizes automatically
mcq.explanation: Dynamic Size: std::string automatically manages its memory. It grows when you add content and shrinks when you remove it. char[] has a FIXED size declared at compile time — dangerous if you exceed it.

coding.title: Native Boolean
coding.objective: What is the C++ boolean type keyword (not Java's 'boolean')?

coding.title: Boolean Value
coding.objective: What is the correct lowercase C++ boolean for true?
coding.hint: All lowercase — same as Java, different from Python's True.

coding.title: Print True as Word
coding.objective: What manipulator makes cout print 'true' instead of '1'?

options: C++ bool uses lowercase: 'true' not 'True' (that's Python)
debugging.answer: C++ bool uses lowercase: 'true' not 'True' (that's Python)
debugging.hint: C++ and Java use lowercase true/false. Python uses capitalized True/False.

options: C++ string type is lowercase: 'string', not 'String' (that's Java)
Alex should be in single quotes
debugging.answer: C++ string type is lowercase: 'string', not 'String' (that's Java)
debugging.hint: In C++, the string type is all lowercase. 'String' (capital S) is Java's class name.

prediction.explanation: bool values are treated as 0/1 in arithmetic. true + false = 1 + 0 = 1. cout prints the integer result.

teaching.title: cin — C++'s Input Stream
teaching.analogy: cin is cout's partner — if cout pushes data OUT to the screen with <<, then cin PULLS data IN from the keyboard with >>. The >> operator extracts data from the input stream and puts it in your variable.
teaching.content: cin (character input stream) reads input from the keyboard. Use >> (extraction operator) to store the input in a variable. Unlike C's scanf, cin automatically handles the type — no format specifiers or & needed!

examples.title: Basic cin Usage
examples.explanation: Much cleaner than C's scanf!

examples.title: cin vs scanf
examples.explanation: No & needed, no format specifiers!

teaching.funFact: cin >> skips whitespace automatically before reading. This is convenient but means reading a full line with spaces requires getline() instead! 🎯

teaching.title: getline() — Reading Full Lines
teaching.analogy: cin >> reads ONE word at a time (stops at spaces). getline() is like a vacuum cleaner — it sucks up the entire line including spaces, until it hits Enter. Use getline() whenever the input might have spaces.
teaching.content: cin >> stops at whitespace — it reads word by word. To read a full line including spaces, use getline(cin, variable). Be careful mixing cin >> and getline() — there's a leftover newline trap!

examples.title: cin >> vs getline
examples.explanation: cin >> reads one word. getline reads the whole line.

examples.title: Mixing cin and getline
examples.explanation: Flush the newline after cin >> before getline.

teaching.funFact: The newline bug when mixing cin >> and getline() is one of the most common C++ beginner mistakes. cin.ignore() is the fix — it discards the leftover '\n' from the input buffer! 🐛

mcq.q: What operator extracts data from cin?
mcq.explanation: Extraction Operator: >> extracts data FROM cin INTO your variable. Remember: cout uses << (insertion), cin uses >> (extraction). Opposite directions!

mcq.q: Does cin >> require format specifiers like scanf?
options: No — cin is type-aware and handles types automatically
mcq.explanation: Type-Safe: cin >> knows the type of your variable at compile time — no %d, %f needed. This is one of C++'s improvements over C's scanf.

mcq.q: Which reads a full name with spaces in C++?
mcq.explanation: Full Line: getline(cin, name) reads until Enter, including spaces. cin >> name would stop at the first space, so 'Alex Smith' would only store 'Alex'.

mcq.q: What does cin.ignore() do?
options: Discards one character (usually the leftover newline) from the input buffer
mcq.explanation: Buffer Clear: cin.ignore() discards the next character in the input buffer — usually the '\n' left by cin >> age. Essential when mixing cin >> with getline() to avoid the newline bug.

coding.title: Read an Integer
coding.objective: What operator reads data from cin into a variable?
coding.hint: The extraction operator — two greater-than signs.

coding.title: Read Full Line
coding.objective: What function reads a full line including spaces?
coding.hint: get + line — gets the entire input line.

coding.title: Fix the Buffer
coding.objective: After 'cin >> age;', what do you call to clear the leftover newline before getline?
coding.hint: It ignores (discards) one character from the buffer.

options: Operators are swapped — cout uses << and cin uses >>
debugging.answer: Operators are swapped — cout uses << and cin uses >>
debugging.hint: cout << sends data out. cin >> pulls data in. Don't swap them!

options: Nothing wrong — it reads Alex Smith
cin >> stops at spaces — use getline(cin, fullName) for full name
fullName is an invalid variable name
debugging.answer: cin >> stops at spaces — use getline(cin, fullName) for full name
debugging.hint: cin >> reads word by word. 'Alex Smith' would only give 'Alex'. Use getline for full lines.

prediction.explanation: cin reads 6 into x. x * x = 6 * 6 = 36. Output: 36.
cin >> stops at the space. Only 'Hello' (5 chars) is stored in s. length() = 5.

teaching.title: Comments in C++
teaching.analogy: Comments in C++ are identical to modern C — // for single-line, /* */ for blocks. C++ programmers typically prefer // for most comments and reserve /* */ for temporarily disabling large blocks of code.
teaching.content: C++ supports all C comment styles: // single-line (preferred) and /* */ multi-line blocks. C++ also introduced Doxygen-style comments (/** */ or ///) for generating API documentation, similar to Java's Javadoc.

examples.title: C++ Comment Styles
examples.explanation: // is the standard; /* */ for multi-line or disabling code.

teaching.funFact: Doxygen is a documentation generator for C, C++, Java, and more. Comments starting with /// or /** */ can automatically generate HTML/PDF documentation from your source code! 📚

teaching.title: C++ Conventions & Best Practices
teaching.analogy: C++ conventions are like street rules for code: some are enforced (syntax) and some are just good manners (naming conventions). Breaking the enforced rules crashes your program; ignoring good manners just annoys your teammates.
teaching.content: C++ naming: variables/functions use camelCase or snake_case (both common), classes use PascalCase, constants use UPPER_SNAKE_CASE. 'using namespace std;' is convenient but avoid it in headers. Prefer const over #define.

examples.title: C++ Naming Conventions
examples.explanation: C++ has more flexible conventions than Java.

examples.title: const vs #define in C++
examples.explanation: Modern C++ strongly prefers const over #define.

teaching.funFact: C++ has had multiple major standards: C++98, C++11, C++14, C++17, C++20. Each adds new features. C++11 was the biggest update (auto, range-for, lambdas, smart pointers). Always check which standard you're compiling with! 📅

mcq.q: Which comment style is preferred in modern C++?
mcq.explanation: C++ Preference: // single-line comments are the standard in modern C++. /* */ is still used for multi-line blocks or temporarily disabling code sections.

mcq.q: Why is 'using namespace std;' avoided in header files?
options: It pollutes the namespace for all files that include that header
mcq.explanation: Namespace Pollution: If a header has 'using namespace std;', every file that includes it gets all std names imported — even if they don't want that. Use std:: explicitly in headers.

mcq.q: Which is preferred in modern C++ for defining constants?
mcq.explanation: Modern Constants: const int (or constexpr) is preferred over #define. const is type-safe, respects scope, and is visible to the debugger. #define is a raw text replacement with no type.

mcq.q: What naming convention do C++ classes use?
mcq.explanation: Class Names: C++ classes use PascalCase — every word starts with uppercase: GameEngine, PlayerScore, DataManager. This is the same as Java's convention.

coding.title: Single Line Comment
coding.objective: What starts a single-line comment in C++?
coding.hint: Same as Java and modern C.

coding.title: Typed Constant
coding.objective: What keyword creates an immutable typed constant in C++?
coding.hint: Preferred over #define — it has a type and respects scope.

coding.title: Explicit Namespace
coding.objective: Without 'using namespace std;', how do you write cout?

options: No space between # and include: must be #include <iostream>
debugging.answer: No space between # and include: must be #include <iostream>
debugging.hint: Preprocessor directives have no space after #. #include not # include.

options: const variables cannot be reassigned — MAX is a constant
debugging.answer: const variables cannot be reassigned — MAX is a constant
debugging.hint: const means constant — you can't change it after initialization. That's the whole point!

prediction.code:
const int x = 5;
cout << x * 2 << endl;
options: Error — const can't be used in expressions
prediction.explanation: const just prevents reassignment — you can still READ and USE the value. x * 2 = 5 * 2 = 10.
The first line is a // comment — compiler ignores it. Only 'Visible' is printed.

teaching.title: ⚠️ BOSS — THE NAMESPACE GUARDIAN
teaching.analogy: The Namespace Guardian is the gatekeeper of the C++ standard library. It controls access to cout, cin, string, and endl. To defeat it, prove you understand not just the syntax, but WHY C++ works the way it does!
teaching.content: THE NAMESPACE GUARDIAN has absorbed all of Level 1's C++ knowledge: #include <iostream>, cout/cin with <</>>, string vs char[], auto type deduction, bool, getline(), and C++ style conventions. Defeat it for your 'First C++ Code' badge!

examples.title: What you've mastered
examples.explanation: Everything from C++ Level 1!
examples.code:
✅ #include <iostream> + using namespace std
✅ cout << with << chaining, endl vs \n
✅ cin >> and getline() for input
✅ int, double, bool, char, string types
✅ auto type deduction
✅ const, // comments, PascalCase

teaching.funFact: C++ compiles to native machine code AND has full access to hardware — yet it also has high-level features like objects, templates, and smart pointers. That's why it powers game engines like Unreal, browsers like Chrome, and databases like MySQL! 🚀

mcq.q:
What is output?
cout << "Code" << "Clash" << endl;
mcq.explanation: Chaining two strings with << concatenates them without spaces. Output: CodeClash.

options: Line 2 — x was deduced as int, can't store string
mcq.explanation: Auto Lock: auto x = 5 deduces x as int. Once deduced, the type is locked — you can't assign a string to an int variable, even with auto.

mcq.q: User types 'Alex Smith'. What does 'cin >> name' store?
mcq.explanation: Word Reading: cin >> reads one word at a time, stopping at whitespace. 'Alex Smith' → only 'Alex' is stored. Use getline(cin, name) for the full name.

mcq.q: What does std::cout mean?
options: cout from the std namespace (used without 'using namespace std;')
mcq.explanation: Scope Resolution: std::cout means 'cout from the std (standard) namespace'. The :: is the scope resolution operator. This is the explicit form, avoiding 'using namespace std;'.

mcq.q: What is the recommended modern C++ alternative to #define PI 3.14?
mcq.explanation: Modern Constant: const double PI = 3.14 is type-safe, scoped, and debugger-visible. #define is a dumb text replacement with no type checking. Always prefer const in C++.

coding.title: Boss Challenge: Complete the Program
coding.objective: What header is needed for cout and cin in C++?

coding.title: Boss Challenge: Read Full Name
coding.objective: Which reads a user's full name including spaces?
coding.hint: cin >> stops at spaces. This function reads the entire line.

options: Missing cin.ignore() between cin >> age and getline — the leftover newline is consumed as the name
name and age order is wrong
debugging.answer: Missing cin.ignore() between cin >> age and getline — the leftover newline is consumed as the name
debugging.hint: cin >> leaves a \n in the buffer. getline immediately reads it as an empty string. Fix: add cin.ignore() after cin >> age.

prediction.explanation: auto a = 10 and auto b = 3 both deduce as int. int / int = integer division. 10 / 3 = 3 (truncated, not 3.33).
